followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: No prior cycle; this is the initial independent review.

main_claims.claim: Self-similar Euclidean quasicrystals can be reformulated via a symmetric cut-and-project scheme tying quasi-periodicity, non-crystallographic symmetry, and discrete scale behavior.
main_claims.notes: The broad framework is standard/credible, but some background Coxeter and scale-factor statements are overgeneralized.

main_claims.claim: The symmetric cut-and-project scheme extends naturally to Lorentzian lattices, with physical spacetime chosen from Coxeter eigenspaces of a hyperbolic Coxeter element.
main_claims.notes: The linear-algebraic recipe is clear in outline. However, total irrationality and model-set regularity are not proved under the stated hypotheses.

main_claims.claim: The paper constructs first explicit Lorentzian spacetime quasicrystal examples from I_{3,1} and II_{9,1}.
main_claims.notes: The examples are specified by matrices/eigenvalues/projectors, but I did not independently verify the computations or the novelty claim. Human spot-check is needed.

main_claims.claim: Lorentzian spacetime quasicrystals have infinite non-crystallographic orientational symmetry, no associated finite symmetric tiling, no local scale invariance, and more natural self-dual/global-scale variants through weighting functions.
main_claims.notes: These are plausible but depend on unstated assumptions about regular windows, finite prototile tilings, and which lattice automorphisms realize scale factors.

main_claims.claim: The II_{9,1} construction suggests a speculative compactified 10D torus picture that could evade closed timelike curve/macroscopic-dimension objections and motivate the electroweak/Planck/vacuum-energy seesaw.
main_claims.notes: The paper labels this as speculative. Several physical steps are heuristic rather than derived.

missing_assumptions.assumption: For every chosen Coxeter eigenspace splitting, E_ph and E_in are totally irrational with respect to the lattice.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Aperiodicity, density of projected modules, injectivity of projection, and the cut-and-project interpretation depend on this property.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Windows and weighting functions are regular enough that invariance under the discrete Coxeter-generated subgroup implies invariance under its closure.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without regularity, the conclusion that invariant windows are disks/radial functions is not valid.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The tiling discussion is restricted to finite-prototile, finite-local-complexity, locally derivable tilings.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The stated no-tiling conclusion is otherwise stronger than the argument supports.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The relevant lattice module is preserved by the full unit group claimed to give scale factors.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Units of a number field need not all produce lattice automorphisms or valid global scale symmetries of the chosen model set.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Matter is exactly confined to the irrational 4D subspace and bulk causal pathologies do not affect brane observers.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is necessary for the claim that the 10D compact Lorentzian torus can appear as noncompact 4D spacetime without closed timelike curves.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the explicit Coxeter elements, eigenvalues, projectors, and displayed quasicrystal data for I_{3,1} and II_{9,1} check out computationally?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The bundle includes many formulas with missing symbols/formatting, and I did not run an independent exact arithmetic verification.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the claim that these are the first Lorentzian/spacetime quasicrystal examples accurate relative to model-set literature and prior Lorentzian lattice constructions?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires a literature-level novelty check beyond the supplied paper.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: For the lattices used, are all claimed number-field units actually realized as automorphisms preserving the chosen splitting?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires explicit arithmetic computation of the lattice module/order and automorphism action.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Can the no-local-scale-invariance statement be made rigorous for the exact class of Lorentzian windows considered?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper gives only a short assertion; a specialist should verify the finite Boolean algebra/window-derivability obstruction.

novelty_assessment: The core idea of using Lorentzian self-dual lattices and hyperbolic Coxeter eigenspaces to build cut-and-project spacetime point sets appears interesting and likely non-routine. The explicit I_{3,1} and II_{9,1} examples may be genuinely novel, but the paper does not fully substantiate the 'first examples' claim against the broader model-set/quasicrystal literature. The physical speculation is novel in flavor but currently heuristic.
score_rationale: The paper has a promising construction and substantial mathematical context, but several local claims are overstated or missing hypotheses. The explicit examples could still be valuable if verified, while the strongest structural and physical conclusions need tightening. I would surface it as interesting/contested rather than technically settled.
summary: The paper proposes a Lorentzian cut-and-project formalism for 'spacetime quasicrystals', using Lorentzian self-dual lattices and Coxeter eigenspace splittings, and gives explicit low-dimensional and 10-dimensional examples. The construction is interesting and plausibly novel, but several supporting claims are stated more strongly than the local arguments justify. The main mathematical examples may be salvageable, but the paper needs clearer hypotheses on irrationality, window regularity, scale-factor arithmetic, and the precise meaning of tiling/no-tiling claims. The physical compactification and hierarchy discussion is explicitly speculative and should not be treated as established evidence.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says the eigenvalues are primitive h-th roots with exponents running over totatives of h. The standard theorem uses the Coxeter exponents m_i, not only integers coprime to h. For example A_3 has h=4 and an eigenvalue -1 corresponding to m=2, which is not a totative of 4.
technical_objections.objection: The finite Coxeter-element eigenvalue statement is incorrect as written.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II.4, paragraph beginning 'For finite W, the eigenvalues of c...'

technical_objections.evidence: The cut-and-project construction requires E_ph and E_in to have no nonzero lattice points. A subgroup being non-crystallographic does not by itself exclude rational invariant summands or lattice vectors in one of the chosen invariant subspaces. The paper should prove total irrationality for each Coxeter eigenspace splitting, e.g. from irreducibility/minimal-polynomial data, rather than state it generically.
technical_objections.objection: The assertion that total irrationality follows from non-crystallographic symmetry is under-justified.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.2.1, final sentence; also used implicitly in Sec. IV.2.2

technical_objections.evidence: The paper infers that an invariant window must be a disk, or an invariant weight must depend only on radial coordinates. This follows from invariance under a dense rotation only with regularity assumptions such as closed/measurable/continuous windows or weights, and with suitable assumptions on angular independence. Arbitrary invariant sets under an irrational rotation can be non-radial orbit unions.
technical_objections.objection: Discrete infinite Coxeter symmetry does not by itself force the window/weight to be radially symmetric in each internal Coxeter plane.

technical_objections.evidence: After citing the local-derivability criterion, the paper asserts that a rescaled ball cannot be expressed by finitely many unions/intersections of translated copies of the original ball. This is plausible for regular Euclidean balls by boundary-curvature reasoning, but the paper gives no proof and does not specify the exact window class, such as balls versus products of disks.
technical_objections.objection: The 'no local scale invariance' conclusion rests on an unproved geometric impossibility claim.

technical_objections.evidence: The argument notes that an infinite symmetry group would generate infinitely many orientations of a tile unless the tile were invariant under a finite-index subgroup. Infinite tile orientations alone do not rule out a tiling; it rules out only a finite-prototile or finite-orientation symmetric tiling under additional assumptions.
technical_objections.objection: The 'point set, but no tiling' argument is too strong without finite-local-complexity/prototile hypotheses.

technical_objections.evidence: Scale factors coming from lattice automorphisms preserving the splitting should correspond to units preserving the relevant lattice/module or order. The paper appears to identify them with the full unit group of the maximal real subfield of the Coxeter splitting field without proving that every such unit is realized by an automorphism of the chosen lattice. The statement that irreducibility of the Coxeter polynomial 'corresponds' to non-crystallographicity is also too coarse.
technical_objections.objection: The arithmetic identification of scale factors with all units of the maximal real subfield is not established.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.3.4 and Appendix B, especially the paragraph defining K and K_R

technical_objections.evidence: The weight w(r)=r^{-(d_in+Delta)} is singular at r=0 and non-integrable there for Delta >= 0, in tension with the earlier finite-average-mass criterion. The text says generic shifts remove the central singular peak and give finite mass in finite regions, but translation-boundedness and average-density behavior require a proof with Diophantine/model-set estimates.
technical_objections.objection: The power-law weighting construction has unresolved analytic conditions.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.3.6, Sec. IV.3.4, Fig. 11 caption

technical_objections.evidence: The claim that particles transforming in a certain representation are confined to the irrational 4D surface does not follow from Wigner-style representation language alone. The finite-thickness dense brane argument also uses a packing formula V_brane V_perp = V_torus despite the fact that a thickening of a dense immersed brane in a compact torus self-overlaps. These are acceptable as speculation but not as derived support.
technical_objections.objection: The physical compactification argument contains unsupported inference steps.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says Type II means the inner product of any two lattice points is an even integer. Standard terminology requires every vector norm to be even; pairwise inner products need not all be even, as E8 roots can have inner product -1. This is likely a wording error but should be corrected.
technical_objections.objection: The definition of an even self-dual lattice is misstated.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II.1, paragraph defining Type II lattices
